perturbation.original_text: Neither party shall disclose the Proprietary Information of the other party to any third party whatsoever by without the prior written approval of the other party and all such information shall be protected with at least the same diligence, care, and precaution that each party uses to protect its own Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither party shall disclose the Proprietary Information of the other party to any third party whatsoever without the prior written approval of the other party and all such information shall be protected with diligence, care, and precaution that each party deems appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase "that each party deems appropriate" introduces ambiguity regarding the standard of care required to protect Proprietary Information. This weakens the obligation, making it subjective rather than objective, and potentially allows a party to claim compliance even with inadequate security measures.
perturbation.law_explanation: VUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy of trade secrets. By stating each party must protect Proprietary Information with the diligence, care, and precaution 'that each party deems appropriate,' the modified text contradicts this requirement as 'deems appropriate' could fall below a 'reasonable' standard, allowing for inadequate protection of trade secrets, in violation of VA Code § 59.1-336, which allows for injunctive relief for actual or threatened misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 59.1-336. Short title and definitions LIS Session Information Bills & Resolutions State Budget Virginia Law Reports to the General Assembly Virginia Law Code of Virginia All Code of Virginia Administrative Code Constitution Charters Authorities Compacts Uncodified Acts Code of Virginia Table of Contents » Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce » Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act » § 59.1-336. Short title and definitions Creating a Report: Check the sections you'd like to appear in the report, then use the "Create Report" button at the bottom of the page to generate your report. Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act 7/13/2025 § 59.1-336 . Short title and definitions. As used in this chapter, which may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, unless the context requires otherwise: "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, use of a computer or computer network without authority, breach of a duty or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation" means: 1. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or 2. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who a. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or b. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was (1) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (2) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (3) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (4) Acquired by accident or mistake. "Person" means a natural pe
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating that each party must protect Proprietary Information with the diligence, care, and precaution 'that each party deems appropriate.' This contradicts the requirement to maintain secrecy of trade secrets, as 'deems appropriate' could fall below a 'reasonable' standard, allowing for inadequate protection of trade secrets, potentially violating VA Code § 59.1-336, which allows for injunctive relief for actual or threatened misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as the 'Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. The changed text, by potentially allowing for inadequate protection, could lead to such misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: With respect to each item of Proprietary Information disclosed, this obligation of the parties under this Agreement shall expire three (3) years from the date of each such disclosure or the maximum period permitted by law if such period is less than three (3) years.
perturbation.changed_text: With respect to each item of Proprietary Information disclosed, this obligation of the parties under this Agreement shall expire approximately three (3) years from the date of each such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause "or the maximum period permitted by law if such period is less than three (3) years" introduces ambiguity and could potentially extend the confidentiality obligations beyond what is legally permissible under Virginia law. The addition of "approximately" adds further ambiguity as to the exact expiration date.
perturbation.law_explanation: VUTSA does not establish a specific outer limit for trade secret protection, but any obligation to maintain secrecy must be reasonable. By removing the limiting clause, the agreement could be interpreted as imposing obligations that extend for an unreasonably long time, thereby violating the spirit of the VUTSA concerning fair competition and misappropriation. A court would determine what is 'reasonable' depending on the trade secret, but failing to consider a lesser period permitted by law puts it at risk.

"Person" means a natural pe
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit consideration of the 'maximum period permitted by law,' which could lead to the confidentiality obligations extending beyond what is legally permissible. The original text ensured compliance by limiting the obligation to three years or the maximum period allowed by law if shorter. The removal of this safeguard, combined with the ambiguity introduced by 'approximately,' creates a potential conflict with VUTSA, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, because it risks imposing obligations for an 'unreasonably long time.' Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' in terms of 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy,' which becomes more susceptible if the secrecy term is unreasonably long. The original contract was explicit in ensuring the obligations did not last longer than the law allowed, the changed contract removed that requirement which directly contradicts the original contracts aim of abiding by the law.

perturbation.original_text: IDO will disclose to INFE such portions of its Proprietary Information solely to enable INFE to determine the desirability of the proposed sale of assets the Placeum.com division of IDO; and INFE shall not disseminate any portions of the Proprietary Information to any third party individual or entity.
perturbation.changed_text: IDO will disclose to INFE such portions of its Proprietary Information solely to enable INFE to potentially consider the proposed sale of assets the Placeum.com division of IDO; and INFE shall try to avoid disseminating any portions of the Proprietary Information to any third party individual or entity.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "determine the desirability" to "potentially consider" weakens the purpose of the disclosure, and changing "shall not disseminate" to "shall try to avoid disseminating" makes the prohibition of disseminating information less strict. The term "try to avoid" is ambiguous, meaning it is unclear how hard the company must work to keep the information secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification allows for a weaker obligation to protect trade secrets. VUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Saying a company will 'try to avoid' disclosure is below the reasonable standard, thus opening the potential for misappropriation of trade secrets without an appropriate defense, as required to prevent injunctive relief as defined under VA Code § 59.1-336.

As used in this chapter, which may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, unless the context requires otherwise: "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, use of a computer or computer network without authority, breach of a duty or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation" means: 1. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or 2. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who a. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or b. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was (1) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (2) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (3) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (4) Acquired by accident or mistake. "Person" means a natural pe
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect proprietary information, creating a potential legal contradiction. The original contract stated that INFE 'shall not disseminate' the information, whereas the modified version states that INFE 'shall try to avoid disseminating' the information. The term 'try to avoid' introduces ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of the obligation. The Law Explanation points out that the VUTSA requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy. Although Snippet 2 is missing, Snippet 1, which discusses 'improper means' of acquiring trade secrets, along with the Law Explanation suggesting that 'try to avoid' does not meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to prevent injunctive relief under VA Code § 59.1-336. Thus the modified text creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: INFE will disclose to IDO such portions of its Proprietary Information solely to enable IDO to determine the desirability of the proposed sale of assets of the Placeum.com division of IDO to INFE; and IDO shall not disseminate any portions of the Proprietary Information to any third party individual or entity.
perturbation.changed_text: INFE may disclose to IDO some portions of its Proprietary Information, as it deems fit, to enable IDO to generally understand the proposed sale of assets of the Placeum.com division of IDO to INFE; and IDO should avoid disseminating any portions of the Proprietary Information to any third party individual or entity.
perturbation.explanation: The modification weakens the disclosure obligation using 'may disclose' and introduces subjectivity using 'as it deems fit'. Additionally, it weakens the non-dissemination obligation from 'shall not disseminate' to 'should avoid disseminating', reducing its enforceability. This creates ambiguity about the amount and type of information to be disclosed and the efforts required to prevent dissemination.
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening the obligation to disclose and prevent dissemination, this change undermines the protection of trade secrets afforded by VUTSA. 'Should avoid disseminating' does not represent a reasonable effort to protect secrecy, potentially leading to misappropriation and making it harder to obtain injunctive relief under VA Code § 59.1-336 should a violation occur.

Uniform Trade Secrets Act 7/13/2025 § 59.1-336 . Short title and definitions. As used in this chapter, which may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, unless the context requires otherwise: "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, use of a computer or computer network without authority, breach of a duty or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. "Person" means a natural pe
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the obligations related to proprietary information disclosure and dissemination. The original text uses 'shall,' indicating a mandatory obligation, while the changed text uses 'may' and 'should avoid,' introducing discretion and reducing the enforceability of these obligations. This directly relates to the protection of trade secrets. Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as the 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' By weakening the language around preventing dissemination, the revised contract increases the risk of misappropriation, thus contradicting the intent to protect trade secrets as outlined in the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, specifically VA Code § 59.1-336. The shift from 'shall not' to 'should avoid' fails to provide a strong enough safeguard against potential disclosure or use, undermining the reasonable efforts required to maintain secrecy and potentially leading to violations of trade secret laws.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed, interpreted, and enforced according to the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia and the parties hereto consent to jurisdiction therein.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be generally construed and interpreted based on principles similar to the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia, and the parties hereto may consider jurisdiction therein.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall be construed, interpreted, and enforced according to the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia" to "shall be generally construed and interpreted based on principles similar to the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia" removes the explicit legal basis for the contract. This introduces ambiguity as to which specific laws govern the agreement. Changing "consent to jurisdiction therein" to "may consider jurisdiction therein" weakens the agreement to be tried in Virginia, creating ambiguity in which state the trial will be located.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia Code § 11-2. Determination of applicable law and forum.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change weakens the choice of law and forum clauses. While not explicitly violating the statute, it introduces ambiguity that could undermine the parties' original intent and create uncertainty in the event of a dispute, which goes against the purpose of VA Code § 11-2, which allows parties to agree on the laws that govern the contracts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 11-2. When written evidence required to maintain action LIS Session Information Bills & Resolutions State Budget Virginia Law Reports to the General Assembly Virginia Law Code of Virginia All Code of Virginia Administrative Code Constitution Charters Authorities Compacts Uncodified Acts Code of Virginia Table of Contents » Title 11. Contracts » Chapter 1. General Provisions » § 11-2. When written evidence required to maintain action Creating a Report: Check the sections you'd like to appear in the report, then use the "Create Report" button at the bottom of the page to generate your report. Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 11. Contracts Chapter 1. General Provisions 7/13/2025 § 11-2 . When written evidence required to maintain action. Unless a promise, contract, agreement, representation, assurance, or ratification, or some memorandum or note thereof, is in writing and signed by the party to be charged or his agent, no action shall be brought in any of the following cases: 1. To charge any person upon or by reason of a representation or assurance concerning the character, conduct, credit, ability, trade, or dealings of another, to the intent or purpose that such other may obtain thereby, credit, money, or goods; 2. To charge any person upon a promise made after attaining the age of majority, to pay a debt contracted during infancy, or upon a ratification after attaining the age of majority, of a promise or simple contract made during infancy; 3. To charge a personal representative upon a promise to answer any debt or damages out of his own estate; 4. To charge any person upon a promise to answer for the debt, default, or misdoings of another; 5. Upon any agreement made upon consideration of marriage; 6. Upon any contract for the sale of real estate, or for the lease thereof for more than a year; 7. Upon any agreement or contract for services to be performed in the sale o
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall be construed, interpreted, and enforced according to the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia" to "shall be generally construed and interpreted based on principles similar to the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia" weakens the governing law provision. Similarly, changing "consent to jurisdiction therein" to "may consider jurisdiction therein" introduces ambiguity regarding jurisdiction. The law explanation states that this introduces ambiguity that could undermine the parties' original intent and create uncertainty, going against the purpose of VA Code § 11-2. Scraped Law Snippet 1, § 11-2, describes when written evidence is required to maintain an action, indicating the importance of clearly defined contractual terms to avoid disputes over enforceability. While the modified text doesn't explicitly violate § 11-2 (which deals with written evidence), it creates an ambiguous legal obligation. The perturbation explanation confirms the changes weaken and introduce ambiguity.
